easy: Water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius at sea level. Ice melts back into water when it gets warm enough.
A triangle has three sides and three angles. The sum of its angles always equals one hundred eighty degrees.
Plants use sunlight to make their own food. This process is called photosynthesis and it also produces oxygen.
The Earth orbits the Sun once every three hundred sixty five days. This journey is what creates one full year.
Even numbers can be divided by two without leaving a remainder. Odd numbers always leave a remainder of one.
Magnets have a north pole and a south pole. Opposite poles attract each other while similar poles repel.
A circle has no corners and no straight sides. Every point on its edge is the same distance from the center.
Gravity pulls objects toward the center of the Earth. This is why a dropped ball always falls downward.
Adding two even numbers always produces another even number. This simple rule works every single time you test it.
The human body has two hundred six bones once it reaches adulthood. Bones support the body and protect its organs.
Sound cannot travel through empty space because it needs a medium such as air or water to move.
Friction is a force that slows down moving objects when two surfaces rub against each other.
A simple machine like a lever helps people lift heavy objects with less effort.
Heat always flows from a warmer object to a cooler one until they reach the same temperature.
Electricity flows through a circuit only when the circuit forms a complete, unbroken loop.
An object at rest stays at rest unless a force acts on it to make it move.
Light travels much faster than sound, which is why thunder is heard after lightning is seen.
A pulley uses a wheel and a rope to make lifting a heavy load easier.
Objects that are denser than water will sink, while less dense objects will float.
A battery stores chemical energy and converts it into electrical energy to power a device.
Waves carry energy from one place to another without moving matter along with them.
A mirror reflects light so that we can see an image of ourselves or other objects.
The three basic states of matter are solid, liquid, and gas.
Static electricity builds up when tiny charged particles collect on the surface of an object.
A thermometer measures temperature by showing how much a liquid inside it expands or shrinks.
An atom is the smallest unit of matter that still keeps the properties of an element.
Water is made of two hydrogen atoms joined to one oxygen atom.
A mixture contains two or more substances that are not chemically combined and can be separated.
Rust forms when iron reacts with oxygen and moisture in the air over time.
Salt dissolves in water because its particles spread evenly throughout the liquid.
Carbon dioxide is a gas that people and animals breathe out with every exhale.
A solution is formed when one substance completely dissolves into another.
Metals are usually shiny, hard, and good at conducting heat and electricity.
An acid tastes sour, while a base often feels slippery to the touch.
Baking soda and vinegar react together to produce bubbles of carbon dioxide gas.
Every substance is made up of tiny particles that are too small to see with the naked eye.
Ice is simply water that has cooled down enough to turn into a solid.
Gold is a metal that does not rust or react easily with air.
A chemical change produces a new substance, unlike a physical change which does not.
Oxygen makes up about twenty one percent of the air we breathe.
The heart pumps blood throughout the body to deliver oxygen and nutrients to every cell.
Lungs allow the body to take in oxygen and release carbon dioxide when breathing.
A seed needs water, warmth, and soil to begin growing into a new plant.
The skeleton gives the human body its shape and protects important internal organs.
Insects have six legs, while spiders have eight legs.
The skin is the largest organ of the human body and helps protect it from germs.
Herbivores eat only plants, while carnivores eat only other animals.
The brain controls the body by sending and receiving signals through the nervous system.
A caterpillar changes into a butterfly through a process called metamorphosis.
Roots absorb water and nutrients from the soil to help a plant grow.
The five basic human senses are sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch.
Fish use gills to take in oxygen from the water around them.
A food chain shows how energy passes from one living thing to another.
Blood carries oxygen from the lungs to every part of the body.
Mammals are animals that have fur or hair and feed their babies with milk.
The solar system has eight planets that all orbit around the Sun.
The Moon does not produce its own light; it only reflects light from the Sun.
A day on Earth lasts about twenty four hours because of how fast our planet spins.
Stars are giant balls of hot gas that produce their own light and heat.
Earth is the third planet from the Sun in our solar system.
The Sun is actually a star, and it is by far the closest one to Earth.
A year on Earth is the time it takes our planet to travel once around the Sun.
Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system.
The night sky looks dark because Earth is facing away from the Sun.
A telescope makes distant objects in space appear closer and easier to see.
Saturn is well known for the large rings of ice and rock that circle around it.
The seasons change because Earth is tilted as it travels around the Sun.
Mars is often called the Red Planet because of the rusty color of its soil.
Astronauts float in space because there is very little gravity pulling them down.
A constellation is a group of stars that seems to form a pattern in the sky.
The water cycle describes how water moves between the ocean, the sky, and the land.
Earthquakes happen when large sections of the Earth's crust suddenly shift and release energy.
A volcano erupts when hot melted rock called magma rises up from below the surface.
Earth's surface is covered mostly by water, with oceans making up most of that area.
Clouds form when tiny drops of water gather together high up in the sky.
Rocks can be classified into three main types: igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic.
Wind is simply air that is moving from one place to another.
A rainbow appears when sunlight passes through raindrops and splits into different colors.
The deepest parts of the ocean are colder and darker than the waters near the surface.
Snow forms when water vapor freezes into ice crystals high up in cold clouds.
Soil is made from tiny pieces of broken rock mixed with decayed plants and other matter.
A desert is a dry region that receives very little rainfall throughout the year.
Lightning is a sudden flash of electricity that happens during a thunderstorm.
Mountains form slowly over a very long time as forces inside the Earth push rock upward.
Fresh water makes up only a small percentage of all the water found on Earth.
A square has four equal sides and four right angles.
Multiplying a number by one always gives back that same number.
A fraction represents a part of a whole, such as one half or one quarter.
Numbers that end in zero, two, four, six, or eight are always even numbers.
The perimeter of a shape is the total distance around its outer edge.
A pentagon is a shape that has exactly five sides and five angles.
Zero is neither a positive number nor a negative number.
Rounding a number means adjusting it to the nearest ten, hundred, or another convenient value.
A number line helps show the order and position of numbers in relation to each other.
Symmetry means that one half of a shape is a mirror image of the other half.
Subtraction is the opposite operation of addition.
A hexagon has six straight sides and six angles.
Place value tells us what a digit is worth based on its position in a number.
Dividing a number by one always gives back that same number.
The area of a shape measures how much flat space it covers.
normal: Learning to type quickly takes regular practice and patience. Focus on accuracy first, and speed will follow naturally over time.
Good habits are built one small step at a time. Consistency matters more than intensity when you are trying to improve any skill.
A clear plan makes even a difficult task feel manageable. Break big goals into smaller pieces you can finish in a single sitting.
Atoms are made of protons, neutrons, and electrons, and the number of protons in an atom determines which chemical element it represents.
In algebra, a variable is a symbol that represents an unknown number, and solving an equation means finding the value that makes it true.
Sound travels through air as a wave, and its speed depends on the temperature and density of the medium it moves through.
The Pythagorean theorem states that in a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides.
Chemical reactions occur when atoms rearrange themselves to form new substances, often releasing or absorbing energy in the process.
A prime number is a whole number greater than one that has no divisors other than one and itself.
Light behaves both as a wave and as a particle, a concept that puzzled scientists for many years before quantum theory explained it.
The speed of an object is calculated by dividing the distance it travels by the amount of time the trip takes.
Cells are the basic building blocks of every living organism, and each one contains structures that carry out specific functions.
In geometry, parallel lines never intersect no matter how far they are extended in either direction across a flat plane.
Newton's first law of motion states that an object will remain at rest or continue moving at a constant velocity unless an external force acts upon it.
Kinetic energy is the energy an object has because of its motion, and it increases as either the object's mass or speed increases.
Potential energy is stored energy based on an object's position, such as a ball held above the ground before it is dropped.
In a series circuit, electricity flows through a single path, so if one part of the circuit breaks, the entire circuit stops working.
The frequency of a wave describes how many complete cycles pass a given point every second, and it is measured in hertz.
Refraction occurs when light bends as it passes from one transparent medium into another, such as from air into water.
Work in physics is done when a force causes an object to move a certain distance in the direction of that force.
Momentum depends on both an object's mass and its velocity, and it is conserved in a closed system during a collision.
Buoyant force pushes an object upward in a fluid, allowing objects less dense than the fluid to float on its surface.
Electromagnetism describes the relationship between electricity and magnetism, since a moving electric charge always produces a magnetic field around it.
Sound intensity is measured in decibels, and even a small increase in decibels can represent a large increase in actual sound energy.
Materials expand when heated because their particles move faster and spread further apart from one another.
Power measures how quickly work is done, so completing the same task in less time requires more power.
In a parallel circuit, electricity has more than one path to follow, so the rest of the circuit keeps working even if one path breaks.
The law of conservation of energy states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form into another.
The periodic table arranges elements into rows and columns based on their atomic number and similar chemical properties.
An ionic bond forms when one atom transfers an electron to another, creating oppositely charged ions that attract each other.
A covalent bond forms when two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons between them.
The pH scale ranges from zero to fourteen, with values below seven considered acidic and values above seven considered basic.
When a substance changes from a liquid to a gas, this process is called evaporation, and it requires energy in the form of heat.
Filtration is a method used to separate solid particles from a liquid by passing the mixture through a fine material.
The concentration of a solution describes how much of a substance is dissolved in a certain amount of liquid.
A catalyst speeds up a chemical reaction without being consumed or permanently changed by the reaction itself.
Oxidation occurs when a substance loses electrons during a chemical reaction, often while combining with oxygen.
Distillation separates liquids with different boiling points by heating a mixture and then condensing the vapor that rises off.
An element cannot be broken down into a simpler substance through ordinary chemical reactions.
A compound forms when two or more elements chemically combine in a fixed ratio, creating a substance with entirely new properties.
Solubility describes how much of a substance can dissolve in a given liquid at a specific temperature.
Endothermic reactions absorb energy from their surroundings, while exothermic reactions release energy into their surroundings.
The atomic number of an element tells you exactly how many protons are found in the nucleus of its atoms.
Mitochondria are often called the powerhouse of the cell because they convert nutrients into usable energy for the cell to function.
Mitosis is the process by which a single cell divides to produce two genetically identical daughter cells.
An ecosystem includes all the living organisms in an area along with the nonliving elements they interact with, such as water and soil.
Genes are segments of DNA that carry the instructions for specific traits, which are passed down from parents to their offspring.
The circulatory system, digestive system, and respiratory system all work together to keep the human body functioning properly.
Enzymes are proteins that speed up chemical reactions inside living cells without being used up in the process.
Homeostasis is the ability of an organism to maintain a stable internal environment despite changes happening outside its body.
Evolution describes how species gradually change over many generations as traits that improve survival become more common.
Taxonomy is the science of classifying living things into groups based on shared characteristics, starting from broad categories down to specific species.
Photosynthesis converts carbon dioxide, water, and sunlight into glucose and oxygen inside the chloroplasts of a plant cell.
The nucleus of a cell contains the organism's genetic material and controls most of the cell's important activities.
A dominant gene will express its trait even if only one copy of it is present, while a recessive gene needs two copies.
Cellular respiration breaks down glucose in the presence of oxygen to release the energy that cells need to function.
Vertebrates are animals that have a backbone, while invertebrates are animals that lack one entirely.
The immune system defends the body against harmful invaders such as bacteria and viruses using specialized cells.
Gravity keeps planets in orbit around the Sun by constantly pulling them toward its massive center.
A light year measures distance, not time, and it represents how far light travels in a single year.
Stars go through a life cycle that includes formation from gas clouds, a stable period of burning fuel, and an eventual dramatic end.
A galaxy is a massive collection of stars, gas, dust, and other matter held together by gravity.
A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon passes directly between the Sun and Earth, briefly blocking the Sun's light.
Ocean tides are caused mainly by the gravitational pull of the Moon acting on the water covering Earth's surface.
Kepler's laws describe how planets move in elliptical, not perfectly circular, orbits around the Sun.
A lunar eclipse happens when Earth passes between the Sun and the Moon, casting its shadow onto the Moon's surface.
The asteroid belt lies between the orbits of Mars and Jupiter and contains countless rocky objects of various sizes.
A comet is made of ice, dust, and rock, and it develops a glowing tail as it nears the Sun.
Neutron stars form when a massive star collapses, packing an enormous amount of mass into an extremely small, dense space.
The Milky Way is the galaxy that contains our entire solar system, along with billions of other stars.
A planet's orbital period is the amount of time it takes to complete one full trip around the Sun.
Space probes are unmanned spacecraft sent to explore planets, moons, and other objects too far or harsh for astronauts to visit.
The atmosphere of a planet can determine its surface temperature by trapping or releasing heat from its star.
Plate tectonics explains how the Earth's crust is broken into large sections that slowly move over the layer beneath them.
The rock cycle describes how rocks transform between igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic forms over long periods of time.
Earth's atmosphere is made up of several distinct layers, including the troposphere, stratosphere, mesosphere, and thermosphere.
Weather refers to short term conditions in the atmosphere, while climate describes long term weather patterns in a particular region.
Erosion gradually wears away rock and soil through the action of wind, water, and ice over time.
Sedimentary rock forms when layers of sediment are compressed and cemented together over an extended period.
An ocean current is a continuous, directed movement of seawater driven by wind, temperature, and salinity differences.
The greenhouse effect describes how certain gases in the atmosphere trap heat from the Sun, warming a planet's surface.
A fault is a fracture in the Earth's crust where blocks of rock have moved relative to one another.
Groundwater is water that has seeped into the ground and collected within layers of soil and rock.
The magnitude of an earthquake is measured using a scale that reflects the amount of energy released at its source.
Metamorphic rock forms when existing rock is transformed by intense heat and pressure without fully melting.
The water table marks the boundary between soil that contains air and soil that is fully saturated with water.
Continental drift describes the slow movement of Earth's continents over millions of years due to tectonic activity.
Humidity measures the amount of water vapor present in the air at a given time.
The area of a rectangle is calculated by multiplying its length by its width.
A ratio compares two quantities and shows how many times one value contains or is contained within the other.
Converting a fraction to a percentage involves dividing the numerator by the denominator and then multiplying the result by one hundred.
Probability measures how likely an event is to occur, expressed as a number between zero and one.
The mean of a data set is found by adding all the values together and dividing by the total number of values.
The volume of a rectangular prism is calculated by multiplying its length, width, and height together.
An exponent tells you how many times a number, called the base, is multiplied by itself.
The order of operations tells you to solve parentheses first, then exponents, then multiplication and division, and finally addition and subtraction.
A linear equation graphs as a straight line, and its steepness is described by a value called the slope.
The coordinate plane uses two number lines, one horizontal and one vertical, to describe the exact position of a point.
A factor of a number divides evenly into it, while a multiple is the product of that number and any whole number.
The greatest common factor is the largest number that divides evenly into two or more given numbers.
hard: Thermal conductivity measures how efficiently a material transfers heat, and metals generally conduct heat far better than materials like wood or plastic because of their free-moving electrons. This is why a metal spoon left in a hot pot quickly becomes too hot to touch, while a wooden spoon in the same pot stays cool enough to hold.

In chemistry, a mole is a unit that represents exactly Avogadro's number of particles, roughly six hundred two sextillion, allowing chemists to relate the microscopic scale of atoms to the macroscopic quantities they can actually measure in a lab. This connection makes it possible to convert between the mass of a substance and the number of individual atoms or molecules it contains.

The Doppler effect explains why an ambulance siren sounds higher in pitch as it approaches and lower as it moves away, because the sound waves are compressed in front of the source and stretched out behind it. Astronomers apply this same principle to starlight, using shifts in its wavelength to determine whether a distant star or galaxy is moving toward or away from Earth.

Thermodynamics describes how energy transforms from one form to another, and the second law states that entropy in an isolated system always tends to increase over time. In practical terms, this means that processes such as ice melting or gas dispersing throughout a room happen spontaneously in one direction, while the reverse almost never occurs on its own.

Calculus allows mathematicians to study continuous change by examining derivatives, which measure the rate at which a function's value shifts relative to its input. For example, if a function describes the position of a moving car over time, its derivative at any moment reveals the car's exact speed at that instant.

Quantum mechanics predicts that subatomic particles can exist in multiple states simultaneously until a measurement forces the system into a single definite outcome. This strange behavior has been confirmed through experiments such as the double slit test, where individual particles create an interference pattern as if they had passed through two openings at once.

The periodic table organizes elements according to increasing atomic number, revealing recurring patterns in electron configuration and chemical behavior across each row. Elements in the same column, called a group, tend to share similar chemical properties because they have the same number of electrons in their outermost shell.

Newton's second law of motion states that the force acting on an object equals its mass multiplied by its acceleration, a relationship expressed as F equals m times a. This means that for the same amount of force, an object with greater mass will accelerate more slowly than one with less mass.

In statistics, standard deviation measures how spread out a set of numbers is from the average, with a larger value indicating greater variability within the data. A data set with a small standard deviation has values clustered tightly around the mean, while one with a large standard deviation has values scattered more widely.

Electromagnetic radiation spans a broad spectrum, ranging from long radio waves to short gamma rays, with visible light occupying only a narrow band in between. Each type of radiation within this spectrum carries a different amount of energy, with gamma rays carrying far more energy per photon than the radio waves used for broadcasting signals.

A differential equation relates a function to its derivatives, and solving one often reveals how a physical system evolves over continuous time. These equations appear throughout science, from modeling how a population of animals grows over time to describing how heat spreads through a metal rod.

DNA replication requires an enzyme called helicase to unwind the double helix before polymerase can synthesize two identical strands from the original template. Once the strands are separated, DNA polymerase adds new complementary nucleotides to each exposed strand, ensuring that every new cell receives an accurate copy of the genetic code.

In linear algebra, a matrix transformation can rotate, scale, or shear a vector space while still preserving the fundamental operations of addition and scalar multiplication. Such transformations are widely used in computer graphics, where a single matrix can rotate, resize, or reposition an object displayed on a screen.

The first law of thermodynamics states that energy within an isolated system is conserved, meaning it can change form but the total amount remains constant over time. This principle explains why a car engine cannot produce more energy than the fuel supplies, since energy is only ever converted between forms such as heat, motion, and chemical potential.

Special relativity shows that the passage of time can differ for observers moving at different speeds relative to one another, an effect known as time dilation. At everyday speeds this effect is far too small to notice, but it becomes significant for objects traveling close to the speed of light, and it has been confirmed using extremely precise atomic clocks.

Wave-particle duality describes how light and matter can exhibit properties of both waves and particles depending on how they are observed and measured. In the famous double slit experiment, light produces an interference pattern typical of waves, yet it also strikes a detector in discrete packets of energy called photons, behaving like individual particles.

Entropy is a measure of disorder within a system, and the second law of thermodynamics states that it tends to increase in any isolated system over time. A shuffled deck of cards is a simple everyday example, since there are vastly more disordered arrangements than ordered ones, making a random shuffle overwhelmingly likely to increase disorder rather than decrease it.

Angular momentum depends on an object's mass, velocity, and distance from its axis of rotation, and it remains conserved unless an external torque is applied. This is why a figure skater spins faster when pulling their arms inward, since reducing their distance from the axis of rotation must be balanced by an increase in rotational speed to keep angular momentum constant.

Semiconductors have electrical conductivity between that of a conductor and an insulator, a property that makes them essential in building transistors and integrated circuits. By adding small amounts of specific impurities in a process called doping, engineers can precisely control how easily a semiconductor conducts electricity, which is the basis for nearly all modern electronic devices.

Nuclear fission releases energy by splitting a heavy atomic nucleus into smaller nuclei, while nuclear fusion releases energy by combining light nuclei into a heavier one. Nuclear power plants rely on controlled fission reactions to generate electricity, while fusion, the same process that powers the Sun, is still being developed as a potential future energy source.

Superconductivity occurs when certain materials, cooled below a critical temperature, conduct electricity with absolutely zero electrical resistance. Because there is no resistance, an electric current can theoretically flow through a superconducting loop indefinitely without losing any energy, a property being explored for highly efficient power transmission.

Maxwell's equations describe how electric and magnetic fields are generated and altered by each other, as well as by electric charges and currents. These four equations unified what were once considered separate phenomena, showing that a changing magnetic field can generate an electric field and vice versa, which is the basis for how generators and transformers work.

The photoelectric effect occurs when light striking a material causes electrons to be ejected, a phenomenon that helped establish the particle nature of light. Notably, increasing the brightness of the light does not eject faster electrons; only increasing the light's frequency does, a result that could not be explained by treating light purely as a wave.

Escape velocity is the minimum speed an object needs to break free from a planet's gravitational pull without any further propulsion. Earth's escape velocity is about eleven kilometers per second, which is why rockets must reach tremendous speeds before their engines can shut off and allow the spacecraft to coast freely through space.

In an electromagnetic wave, oscillating electric and magnetic fields travel perpendicular to each other and to the direction the wave itself is moving. Because these waves do not require a physical medium to travel through, they can move through the vacuum of space, which is how sunlight reaches Earth across millions of kilometers of empty space.

The uncertainty principle states that it is impossible to simultaneously know both the exact position and exact momentum of a subatomic particle. This is not a limitation of measuring instruments but a fundamental property of nature itself, meaning that gaining more certainty about a particle's position necessarily increases the uncertainty in its momentum.

Doppler shift describes how the observed frequency of a wave changes when its source is moving relative to the observer, either compressing or stretching the wave. This same effect explains why an ambulance siren sounds higher in pitch as it approaches and lower as it drives away, since the sound waves are compressed ahead of it and stretched out behind it.

A blackbody radiates energy across a continuous spectrum of wavelengths determined entirely by its temperature, a principle that led to the development of quantum theory. Attempts to explain blackbody radiation using classical physics failed at short wavelengths, a problem known as the ultraviolet catastrophe, which was only resolved once energy was understood to be emitted in discrete packets called quanta.

Molecular orbital theory describes how atomic orbitals combine to form new orbitals that are shared across an entire molecule rather than belonging to a single atom. Unlike simpler models that treat bonds as belonging to just two atoms, molecular orbital theory can explain more complex behavior, such as why molecular oxygen is attracted to magnetic fields.

The equilibrium constant expresses the ratio of product concentrations to reactant concentrations once a reversible reaction has reached a stable, balanced state. A large equilibrium constant indicates that the reaction favors the formation of products, while a small one indicates that the reactants are favored once the system settles into balance.

Activation energy is the minimum amount of energy required to start a chemical reaction, and catalysts work by lowering this energy barrier. Even reactions that release energy overall still require an initial input of activation energy to get started, much like a ball resting in a small dip must first be pushed over a rim before it can roll downhill.

Electronegativity increases across a period and decreases down a group on the periodic table, reflecting how strongly an atom attracts shared electrons. Fluorine has the highest electronegativity of any element, which is why it forms extremely strong and stable bonds when it combines with other atoms.

A redox reaction involves the simultaneous transfer of electrons, with one substance being oxidized while another is reduced. A helpful way to remember this is the phrase oxidation is loss and reduction is gain, referring to the transfer of electrons between the two substances involved.

A buffer solution resists changes in pH by neutralizing small additions of acid or base, typically using a weak acid paired with its conjugate base. Blood in the human body is a natural example of a buffer system, helping to keep its pH within a very narrow and stable range despite the many acidic and basic substances introduced through diet and metabolism.

Enthalpy measures the total heat content of a system at constant pressure, and its change during a reaction indicates whether the process releases or absorbs heat. A reaction that releases heat into its surroundings has a negative enthalpy change, while one that absorbs heat from its surroundings has a positive enthalpy change.

Stoichiometry uses the ratios established by a balanced chemical equation to calculate the exact amounts of reactants and products involved in a reaction. If a recipe were treated like a chemical equation, stoichiometry would be the tool used to scale the ingredients up or down while keeping their relative proportions exactly correct.

Isotopes of an element share the same number of protons but differ in their number of neutrons, giving them different atomic masses. Carbon-12 and carbon-14 are both isotopes of carbon, but carbon-14 is unstable and decays at a known rate, which is why it can be used to estimate the age of ancient organic materials.

Le Chatelier's principle predicts that a system at equilibrium will shift to counteract any change in concentration, temperature, or pressure imposed upon it. For example, increasing the pressure on a gas-phase reaction at equilibrium will shift the balance toward whichever side has fewer total gas molecules, partially relieving that added pressure.

Radioactive decay occurs when an unstable atomic nucleus loses energy by emitting radiation, gradually transforming into a more stable element over time. Each radioactive isotope has a characteristic half life, the time it takes for half of a given sample to decay, which can range from fractions of a second to billions of years depending on the element.

Molarity expresses the concentration of a solution as the number of moles of solute dissolved per liter of solution. A solution with a molarity of one mole per liter contains exactly one mole of dissolved solute for every liter of total solution volume, making molarity a precise way to compare concentrations.

Van der Waals forces are weak intermolecular attractions that arise from temporary shifts in electron distribution around otherwise neutral molecules. Although individually weak, these forces become significant in large molecules and are responsible for phenomena such as geckos being able to cling to smooth vertical surfaces using microscopic hairs on their feet.

A precipitate forms when two aqueous solutions react to produce an insoluble solid that separates out of the resulting mixture. Observing a precipitate form is one of the simplest visual clues that a chemical reaction has actually taken place, since a new solid appearing where none existed before signals that new substances have been created.

Hess's law states that the total enthalpy change of a reaction is the same regardless of whether it occurs in a single step or several steps. This principle allows chemists to calculate the enthalpy change of a reaction that would be difficult to measure directly by instead adding together the enthalpy changes of a series of simpler, already known reactions.

During transcription, an enzyme called RNA polymerase copies a segment of DNA into a strand of messenger RNA, which then carries the genetic code out of the nucleus. This process is tightly regulated, since different genes are turned on or off depending on what proteins a particular cell needs to produce at any given time.

Cellular respiration includes a stage called the Krebs cycle, which extracts energy from glucose derivatives to produce molecules that fuel the final energy-generating steps. The molecules generated by the Krebs cycle are then used in a later stage called the electron transport chain, which produces the majority of the usable energy a cell obtains from a single glucose molecule.

Photosynthesis consists of light dependent reactions, which capture solar energy, and light independent reactions, which use that energy to build glucose from carbon dioxide. The oxygen released during the light dependent reactions is actually a byproduct of splitting water molecules, and it is this same oxygen that is released into the atmosphere for other organisms to breathe.

Natural selection favors traits that improve an organism's chance of survival and reproduction, gradually shifting the characteristics of a population across many generations. Individuals with traits better suited to their environment tend to survive longer and produce more offspring, passing those advantageous traits on to the next generation more frequently than individuals without them.

A genetic mutation is a change in a DNA sequence that can arise spontaneously or be caused by exposure to certain chemicals or radiation. While many mutations have little to no effect, some can alter how a protein functions, and a small number may even provide an advantage that natural selection can act upon over time.

During translation, ribosomes read the sequence of messenger RNA in groups of three nucleotides called codons to assemble a specific chain of amino acids into a protein. Each codon corresponds to a specific amino acid or signals the ribosome to stop, meaning the exact sequence of codons ultimately determines the precise order of amino acids in the finished protein.

Trophic levels describe the position an organism occupies in a food chain, with energy decreasing significantly as it transfers from one level to the next. On average, only about ten percent of the energy at one trophic level is passed on to the next, which is why food chains rarely extend beyond four or five levels.

Homeostasis is maintained through feedback loops, in which the body detects a change from its normal state and triggers a response to restore balance. Regulating body temperature is a common example, since sweating cools the body down when it becomes too warm, while shivering generates heat when the body becomes too cold.

Plate tectonics is driven largely by convection currents within the Earth's mantle, which slowly move the rigid plates that make up the planet's crust. Where two plates meet, they may collide to form mountain ranges, slide past one another to cause earthquakes, or pull apart to create new ocean floor as magma rises to fill the gap.

Antibodies are proteins produced by the immune system that specifically recognize and bind to foreign substances called antigens, marking them for destruction. Each antibody is shaped to match a specific antigen much like a key fits a particular lock, which allows the immune system to target invaders with remarkable precision.

Meiosis produces four genetically distinct reproductive cells, each containing half the number of chromosomes found in the original parent cell. This reduction in chromosome number is essential for sexual reproduction, since it ensures that when two reproductive cells combine, the resulting offspring has the normal, complete number of chromosomes rather than double that amount.

An allele is one of two or more versions of a gene, and the specific combination of alleles an organism carries determines many of its observable traits. When an organism inherits two different alleles for the same gene, the dominant allele is typically the one whose trait is expressed, while the recessive allele's trait remains hidden unless inherited from both parents.

Osmosis is the movement of water across a semipermeable membrane from an area of lower solute concentration to an area of higher solute concentration. This process is why a plant's cells become firm when well watered, and why they wilt when the surrounding soil becomes too dry for water to move into the roots.

Speciation occurs when populations of the same species become reproductively isolated from one another and accumulate enough genetic differences to form distinct species. Geographic barriers such as mountain ranges or bodies of water often drive this isolation, preventing separated populations from interbreeding long enough for them to evolve into genetically distinct species.

Enzyme activity is influenced by temperature and pH, and each enzyme typically has an optimal range in which it functions most efficiently. Beyond its optimal range, an enzyme's structure can become distorted in a process called denaturation, which usually causes it to lose its ability to function properly.

Stellar nucleosynthesis is the process by which stars fuse lighter elements into heavier ones within their cores, producing much of the matter found in the universe. Elements heavier than iron are typically formed only in the extreme conditions of a dying star's final explosion, meaning that much of the material making up planets and living things was originally forged inside stars.

A black hole forms when a massive star collapses under its own gravity into a region so dense that not even light can escape its pull. The boundary surrounding a black hole beyond which nothing can escape is called the event horizon, and its size depends directly on how much mass the black hole contains.

The cosmic microwave background is the faint radiation left over from the early universe, and it provides strong evidence supporting the Big Bang theory. This radiation is remarkably uniform in every direction across the sky, but its tiny temperature variations correspond to the seeds from which galaxies and large scale cosmic structures eventually formed.

Redshift occurs when light from a distant galaxy stretches toward longer wavelengths as that galaxy moves away, a key piece of evidence for an expanding universe. The greater a galaxy's redshift, the faster it appears to be moving away from Earth, which is one of the key observations that led scientists to conclude the universe is expanding.

General relativity describes gravity not as a force but as the curvature of spacetime caused by the presence of mass and energy. A massive object such as a star bends the surrounding fabric of spacetime, and other objects moving nearby follow the resulting curved paths, which is what we perceive and describe as gravitational attraction.

Astronomers detect exoplanets using methods such as observing the tiny dip in a star's brightness as a planet passes in front of it. This method, called the transit technique, can also reveal information about a planet's size and orbital period based on how much light is blocked and how often the dimming repeats.

A supernova is the explosive death of a massive star, briefly outshining an entire galaxy and scattering heavy elements into surrounding space. The shockwave and material ejected by a supernova can trigger the formation of new stars in nearby clouds of gas and dust, linking the death of one star to the birth of others.

The Hubble constant describes the rate at which the universe is expanding, relating a galaxy's distance to the speed at which it is receding. A higher Hubble constant would imply a younger, faster expanding universe, so refining this value remains an active and important area of ongoing astronomical research.

Dark matter does not emit, absorb, or reflect light, yet its gravitational effects on visible matter reveal that it makes up a large portion of the universe's mass. Galaxies rotate at speeds that could not be explained by the gravity of their visible matter alone, which is one of the strongest pieces of evidence that led astronomers to propose the existence of dark matter.

A pulsar is a rapidly rotating neutron star that emits beams of radiation, which appear to pulse as they sweep past an observer on Earth. Because pulsars rotate with extremely precise and predictable timing, astronomers sometimes use them as natural clocks to test theories of gravity and to help detect ripples in spacetime known as gravitational waves.

Gravitational lensing occurs when the gravity of a massive object bends and magnifies the light from another object positioned farther behind it. This effect can even produce multiple visible images or a ring-shaped distortion of a single distant galaxy, depending on how precisely the foreground and background objects are aligned.

The habitable zone around a star is the range of orbital distances where conditions could allow liquid water to exist on a planet's surface. A planet's distance from its star is not the only factor, since its atmosphere, size, and composition also play major roles in determining whether liquid water could actually exist on its surface.

Binary star systems consist of two stars that orbit a common center of mass, bound together by their mutual gravitational attraction. Studying the orbits of binary star systems allows astronomers to calculate the mass of each star directly, since the way two objects orbit each other depends predictably on their combined gravitational pull.

Cosmic inflation proposes that the very early universe underwent an extremely rapid expansion before settling into the slower expansion observed today. This brief but extreme expansion is thought to explain why the universe appears remarkably uniform on the largest scales, even between regions that would not otherwise have had time to interact.

Spectroscopy allows astronomers to determine the composition, temperature, and motion of distant stars by analyzing the specific wavelengths of light they emit. By comparing the specific pattern of absorbed or emitted wavelengths to those produced by known elements in a laboratory, astronomers can identify exactly which elements are present in a star many light years away.

A derivative measures the instantaneous rate of change of a function at a specific point, calculated as the limit of the average rate of change over a shrinking interval. Derivatives are especially useful for finding maximum and minimum values of a function, since these points occur exactly where the derivative equals zero.

An integral calculates the accumulated area under a curve, and it can be understood as the reverse operation of taking a derivative. A common application is calculating the total distance traveled by an object when only its changing speed over time is known, since integrating speed with respect to time yields the distance covered.

A limit describes the value a function approaches as its input gets arbitrarily close to a particular point, even if the function is undefined exactly at that point. Limits are essential for defining both derivatives and integrals rigorously, since both concepts rely on examining what happens as an interval shrinks toward zero rather than ever actually reaching it.

Eigenvalues and eigenvectors describe how a linear transformation stretches or compresses space along specific directions without changing those directions themselves. These concepts are widely used in fields such as engineering and data science, including techniques that reduce large, complex data sets down to their most important underlying patterns.

A differential equation expresses a relationship between a function and its derivatives, and solving it reveals how a system changes continuously over time. Many differential equations cannot be solved with a simple formula, so scientists and engineers often rely on numerical methods to approximate how the system behaves over time.

Standard deviation quantifies how much individual data points typically differ from the mean of a data set, with a higher value indicating greater spread. In a normal distribution, roughly sixty eight percent of all data points fall within one standard deviation of the mean, a relationship that statisticians rely on constantly.

Linear regression fits a straight line through a set of data points in a way that minimizes the overall distance between the line and each point. Once fitted, the resulting line can be used to estimate an output value for a new input that was not part of the original data set, provided the underlying relationship remains roughly linear.

Hypothesis testing uses sample data to determine whether there is enough statistical evidence to reject an assumption made about a larger population. If the observed data would be extremely unlikely under the original assumption, researchers reject that assumption in favor of an alternative explanation supported by the evidence.

Prime factorization breaks a number down into the unique set of prime numbers that, when multiplied together, produce the original number. Every whole number greater than one has exactly one prime factorization, a fact known as the fundamental theorem of arithmetic, which underlies much of modern number theory.

Modular arithmetic deals with remainders after division, wrapping numbers around once they reach a fixed value called the modulus. A familiar everyday example is a twelve hour clock, where adding hours wraps back around to one after reaching twelve, following exactly the same logic as modular arithmetic.

Combinatorics is the branch of mathematics concerned with counting, arranging, and combining objects according to specific rules. These counting techniques are essential for calculating probabilities, since determining the likelihood of an event often first requires knowing exactly how many possible outcomes exist in total.

A complex number consists of a real part and an imaginary part, the latter involving the square root of negative one. Although they may seem abstract, complex numbers are essential tools in fields such as electrical engineering, where they simplify calculations involving alternating current circuits.

Trigonometric identities are equations involving sine, cosine, and tangent that hold true for every possible value of the variable involved. These identities allow a complicated trigonometric expression to be rewritten in a simpler equivalent form, which is often the key step in solving more advanced equations in calculus and physics.

Vector calculus extends the ideas of derivatives and integrals to vector fields, describing quantities that have both magnitude and direction at every point in space. This branch of mathematics is essential for describing physical phenomena such as fluid flow and electromagnetic fields, where the strength and direction of a quantity can vary from point to point in space.

Topology studies properties of shapes that remain unchanged under continuous deformations such as stretching or bending, without cutting or gluing. A classic example is that a coffee mug and a donut are considered topologically identical, since one shape can be continuously deformed into the other without any cutting or tearing.

A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers that can represent a linear transformation, and multiplying two matrices combines their transformations into one. Multiplying a vector by a matrix can rotate, stretch, or otherwise reshape that vector, which is why matrices are fundamental to fields ranging from computer graphics to solving systems of linear equations.

The determinant of a matrix is a single number that reveals whether the matrix can be inverted and how it scales area or volume. A determinant of zero indicates that the matrix cannot be inverted, which corresponds geometrically to the transformation collapsing space into a lower dimension.

A Taylor series approximates a function as an infinite sum of terms calculated from the function's derivatives at a single point. By including more terms from the series, the approximation becomes increasingly accurate near the chosen point, which makes Taylor series a powerful tool for estimating complicated functions.

In graph theory, a graph consists of vertices connected by edges, and it can be used to model relationships between distinct objects. This framework is widely applied beyond mathematics, including modeling social networks, mapping transportation routes, and helping search engines determine how web pages are connected to one another.

A recursive sequence defines each term based on one or more of the terms that came before it. The Fibonacci sequence is a well known example, where each number is defined as the sum of the two numbers that came directly before it.

The binomial theorem provides a formula for expanding an expression raised to a whole number power without multiplying it out term by term. This shortcut becomes especially valuable for higher powers, where manually multiplying an expression by itself many times would otherwise be extremely tedious and prone to error.

A function is continuous at a point if its value at that point matches the limit approached from both directions around it. Intuitively, a continuous function can be drawn without ever lifting a pen from the paper, since there are no sudden jumps, breaks, or holes anywhere along its graph.

Vector spaces are mathematical structures in which vectors can be added together and scaled by numbers while following a consistent set of rules. Many familiar mathematical objects, including ordinary geometric vectors, polynomials, and even certain functions, can all be shown to form valid vector spaces under the right operations.

A confidence interval provides a range of values that is likely to contain an unknown population parameter based on sample data. A ninety five percent confidence interval means that if the same sampling process were repeated many times, about ninety five percent of the resulting intervals would be expected to contain the true population value.

The chain rule allows the derivative of a composite function to be calculated by multiplying the derivatives of its individual parts. This rule is essential whenever one quantity depends on a second quantity that itself depends on a third, allowing the overall rate of change to be broken down into more manageable individual steps.

A logarithm answers the question of what exponent a fixed base must be raised to in order to produce a given number. Logarithms are especially useful for working with very large or very small numbers, since they compress a wide range of values into a much more manageable scale, as seen with the Richter scale used for earthquakes.

Partial derivatives measure how a function with multiple variables changes as only one of those variables is adjusted while the others stay fixed. These are essential tools in fields such as economics and physics, where outcomes typically depend on several changing variables at once rather than just a single one.
